# Developing Ai Tailored Immunocytokines To Target Melanoma Brain Metastases
## Research Significance
Brain metastases (BMs) present a formidable challenge in cancer treatment, significantly impacting patient survival and quality of life.   Melanoma BMs, occurring in nearly half of metastatic melanoma patients, carry a particularly dismal prognosis, underscoring a critical need for more effective therapeutic strategies. The brain's unique microenvironment, characterized by the blood-brain barrier and inherent immunosuppressive mechanisms, contributes to the difficulty in treating BMs.   Current therapeutic options, including surgery, radiation, and systemic therapies, often prove inadequate due to limited efficacy and significant toxicity. The immunosuppressive nature of the BM tumor microenvironment (TME) hinders the effectiveness of immunotherapies, which have shown remarkable success in other cancer types.   This project directly addresses this critical barrier by developing AI-tailored immunocytokines designed to penetrate the BM TME and reactivate anti-tumor immune responses.   This research builds upon recent advances in single-cell technologies, AI-driven analysis, and protein engineering to overcome the limitations of current approaches.   Existing research has provided valuable insights into the cellular composition and molecular characteristics of the BM TME.   However, these studies have largely focused on individual aspects of the TME, lacking a holistic, multimodal approach. Moreover, current immunotherapy strategies have had limited success in melanoma BMs due to the immunosuppressive TME.   This project hypothesizes that a comprehensive, AI-driven analysis of the BM TME, combined with the development of targeted immunocytokines, will offer a transformative approach to treating melanoma BMs. Aim 1 of this project will establish detailed BM TME models using cutting-edge single-cell technologies, including Zman-seq for temporal immune profiling, PIC-seq for cell-cell interaction analysis, and Stereo-seq for spatial distribution mapping. These data will be integrated using AI-driven tools to identify key therapeutic targets and predict responses to cytokine modulation.   Aim 2 will utilize these targets to conduct a preclinical screening of cytokines in orthotopic immunocompetent BM models.   This screening will identify potent cytokines capable of shifting the BM TME from an immunologically “cold” state to a “hot” state, thereby enhancing anti-tumor activity.   Finally, Aim 3 will design tumor-targeting immunocytokines by incorporating the identified cytokines into antibody-cytokine fusion proteins. These immunocytokines will be designed to target species-conserved tumor antigens highly expressed in melanoma BMs, ensuring precise delivery and maximizing therapeutic efficacy.   This integrated approach, combining detailed TME modeling, cytokine screening, and targeted immunocytokine design, represents a significant departure from conventional BM treatment strategies. By leveraging AI-driven analysis and advanced protein engineering, this project aims to overcome the limitations of existing therapies and translate research findings into tangible clinical benefits for patients with melanoma BMs.   The successful development of these AI-tailored immunocytokines could revolutionize the treatment landscape for melanoma BMs, offering new hope for improved patient outcomes and ultimately extending survival.
## Research Innovation
This project presents a highly innovative approach to treating melanoma brain metastases (BMs) by developing AI-tailored immunocytokines designed to penetrate the BM TME and reactivate anti-tumor immune responses.   The project's novelty lies in its integrated, multimodal approach. Current research has provided valuable insights into individual aspects of the BM TME but lacks a holistic, multimodal analysis.   This project addresses this gap by combining cutting-edge single-cell technologies, AI-driven analysis, and protein engineering to overcome limitations of current approaches.   Specifically, the innovative use of Zman-seq for temporal immune profiling, PIC-seq for cell-cell interaction analysis, and Stereo-seq for spatial distribution mapping provides an unprecedentedly detailed characterization of the BM TME.   This deep characterization is critical for identifying key therapeutic targets and developing targeted therapies. The AI-driven integration of these data enables a comprehensive understanding of the BM TME complexity and facilitates the identification of potential therapeutic targets and the prediction of responses to cytokine modulation, exceeding the capabilities of traditional analysis methods.   This approach represents a significant departure from conventional BM treatment strategies, which often prove inadequate due to limited efficacy and significant toxicity.   Furthermore, the project challenges current immunotherapy strategies by directly addressing the immunosuppressive nature of the BM TME, a major hurdle for effective immunotherapy in melanoma BMs.   The project's preclinical screening of cytokines in orthotopic immunocompetent BM models further distinguishes it from previous research.   This rigorous screening process, coupled with AI-driven analysis, identifies potent cytokines capable of shifting the BM TME from an immunologically “cold” state to a “hot” state, crucial for enhancing anti-tumor activity and immunotherapy efficacy.    The design and development of tumor-targeting immunocytokines by incorporating identified cytokines into antibody-cytokine fusion proteins represent another major innovation.   These immunocytokines, targeting species-conserved tumor antigens highly expressed in melanoma BMs, ensure precise delivery and maximize therapeutic efficacy while minimizing off-target effects.   The use of advanced protein design techniques, including the selection of peptide linkers, blocking moieties, and tumor-associated macrophage (TAM)-specific cleavage sites, further optimizes the immunocytokine design for targeted delivery and controlled release within the BM TME.   The project's commitment to rigorous in vitro and in vivo validation of these immunocytokines, using advanced assays like SPR, ELISA, cell-based binding, reporter assays, and co-culture assays with huMDMs, NK or T cells, ensures their efficacy and safety before potential clinical translation.   This iterative optimization approach, driven by preclinical data and AI analysis, moves beyond the traditional response/no-response paradigm in melanoma BM treatment, aiming for model-driven optimization to pinpoint the most effective constructs for Investigational New Drug (IND) applications.   In summary, this project pioneers a transformative approach by integrating state-of-the-art technologies with AI-driven analysis.   The development of AI-tailored immunocytokines holds the potential to revolutionize the treatment landscape for melanoma BMs, offering new hope for improved patient outcomes and ultimately extending survival.
## Specific Aims
The long-term goal of this research is to revolutionize the treatment of melanoma brain metastases (BMs), which currently carry a dismal prognosis, by developing AI-tailored immunocytokines. This project addresses the critical gap in current BM treatment strategies, which often prove inadequate due to the immunosuppressive nature of the brain microenvironment and the limitations of existing therapies.   Specifically, this research lacks a holistic, multimodal approach to understanding and targeting the complex interplay between tumor cells and the immune system within the BM tumor microenvironment (TME).   This project aims to overcome these limitations through three Specific Aims. Aim 1: Develop BM TME models with holistic, multimodal AI-driven analysis. This aim hypothesizes that creating detailed BM TME models through advanced single-cell technologies and AI-driven analysis, including Zman-seq for temporal immune profiling, PIC-seq for cell-cell interaction analysis, and Stereo-seq for spatial distribution mapping, will reveal key therapeutic targets for antibody and cytokine modulation of immune activity.   This comprehensive characterization of the BM TME will lay the groundwork for the development of targeted immunotherapies. Aim 2: Conduct preclinical screening of cytokines in orthotopic immunocompetent BM models. This aim hypothesizes that specific cytokines can modulate the immune activity within the BM TME to enhance anti-tumor activity.   This aim will involve screening a library of cytokines in vitro and in vivo to identify those capable of shifting the BM TME from an immunologically "cold" to a "hot" state, thereby enhancing the efficacy of immunotherapies.   Aim 3: Design tumor-targeting immunocytokines. This aim hypothesizes that potent cytokines identified in Aim 2 can be incorporated into antibody-cytokine fusion proteins (immunocytokines) targeting species-conserved tumor antigens highly expressed in melanoma BMs.   This aim will involve the design, production, and in vitro validation of immunocytokine candidates, optimizing their structure and function for targeted delivery and controlled release within the BM TME. Successful completion of these aims will significantly advance the field by providing a deeper understanding of the complex interactions within the BM TME, identifying potent cytokines for immunotherapy, and developing novel AI-tailored immunocytokines for targeted therapy. This innovative approach has the potential to transform the treatment landscape for melanoma BMs, offering new hope for improved patient outcomes and ultimately extending survival.   The results of this research, regardless of individual aim outcomes, will provide valuable insights into BM TME biology and immunomodulation, informing future research and therapeutic development.   Each aim has clear endpoints, including the development of detailed BM TME models, identification of potent cytokines, and the creation of optimized immunocytokine candidates, enabling reviewers to readily assess the project's success.
## Research Plan
### Research Aims
#### Aim 1: Developing BM TME models with holistic, multimodal AI-driven analysis
This research aim, "Developing BM TME models with holistic, multimodal AI-driven analysis," hypothesizes that creating detailed BM TME models through advanced single-cell technologies and AI-driven analysis will reveal targets for antibody and cytokine modulation of immune activity within the brain, enhancing anti-tumor activity. The overarching goal of this aim is to comprehensively characterize the immune landscape within the BM TME, identify key cellular interactions and spatial distributions, and pinpoint potential therapeutic targets. This aim will employ a methodology consisting of temporal immune profiling, cell-cell interaction analysis, and spatial distribution mapping. Specifically, Zman-seq will track immune temporal changes in the BM TME, leveraging existing glioma research. PIC-seq will measure immune cell interactions within the BM TME, providing a deeper understanding of the dynamics within this microenvironment. Stereo-seq will analyze the spatial distribution of immune cells within the BM TME. The expected results of this aim include a detailed temporal map of immune activity, an in-depth understanding of cell-cell interactions, and a comprehensive spatial map of immune cell distributions in the BM TME. These results, when analyzed with AI-driven tools, are anticipated to reveal specific targets for antibody and cytokine therapies to shift the brain's immune activity towards a more anti-tumor state. The relations array for this research aim is empty, indicating no direct dependencies or interactions with other research aims.
##### Research Tasks
##### Task 1.1: Temporal understanding of immune activity in BM TME
This research task, "Temporal understanding of immune activity in BM TME," aims to investigate the dynamic changes in immune cell populations and their functional states within the bone marrow tumor microenvironment (BM TME) using Zman-seq.   Building upon our prior work adapting Zman-seq from glioma to the BM TME, this study will provide high-resolution temporal data on immune cell behavior. The experimental design will involve collecting BM TME samples at multiple time points post-tumor induction.   Zman-seq, a combination of single-cell RNA sequencing and temporal barcoding, will be employed to capture the transcriptional profiles of individual immune cells and reconstruct their lineage relationships over time. This approach allows us to track the emergence, differentiation, and functional changes of various immune cell types within the BM TME, revealing the complex interplay between tumor cells and the immune system. The data collected using Zman-seq will be analyzed using bioinformatics tools to identify key transcriptional changes in immune cells over time.   Trajectory inference algorithms will be employed to reconstruct the developmental pathways of different immune cell lineages and determine their functional states at each time point. We will assess the changes in expression of genes associated with immune activation, suppression, and exhaustion, and investigate how these changes correlate with tumor progression. The findings will provide valuable insights into the temporal dynamics of immune responses in the BM TME, potentially revealing critical time windows for therapeutic intervention.
##### Task 1.2: Immune cell-cell interaction in the BM TME
This research task, "Immune cell-cell interaction in the BM TME," aims to comprehensively characterize the interplay between immune cells within the bone marrow tumor microenvironment using the Proximity-Induced Co-localization Sequencing (PIC-seq) technology.   PIC-seq allows for the identification and quantification of cell-cell interactions by detecting the co-localization of barcoded antibodies bound to the surface of interacting cells.   By analyzing the frequency and specificity of these interactions, we will gain crucial insights into the complex communication networks that govern immune responses within the BM TME. This approach will provide a detailed map of the cellular interactions, revealing which immune cell populations interact with each other and the strength of these interactions.   This data will be crucial for understanding how immune cells coordinate their functions and how these interactions influence tumor growth and progression.   The data generated from PIC-seq will be analyzed using computational pipelines specifically designed to process proximity ligation data.   These pipelines will identify significant cell-cell interactions by comparing the observed co-localization frequencies with those expected by chance.   We will then integrate this interaction data with other datasets generated within this project, including single-cell RNA sequencing data and spatial transcriptomics data, to develop a comprehensive understanding of the cellular composition, spatial organization, and functional states of immune cells in the BM TME.   This integrated analysis will enable us to identify key interactions that drive anti-tumor immunity or contribute to immune suppression.   Specifically, we will investigate interactions between T cells, myeloid cells, and other immune populations to determine their role in shaping the immune landscape of the BM TME. The relations array for this research task is empty.
##### Task 1.3: immune spatial distribution in the BM TME
This research task (1.3) aims to characterize the spatial distribution of immune cells within the bone marrow tumor microenvironment (BM TME) using stereo-seq.   This advanced spatial transcriptomics technology allows for high-resolution mapping of gene expression within the TME, providing insights into the complex interactions between tumor cells and immune cells.   The experimental design involves collecting BM samples from the established orthotopic immunocompetent murine models for melanoma BMs and the cohort of human patient samples. The samples will be processed using the stereo-seq protocol.   The data generated will consist of high-resolution spatial transcriptomics data, providing information about the location and expression levels of genes within the BM TME. This methodology enables us to identify specific immune cell populations and their spatial organization, including infiltrating lymphocytes, myeloid-derived suppressor cells, and tumor-associated macrophages. Data analysis will involve processing and quality control of the raw stereo-seq data, followed by clustering analysis to identify spatially distinct regions within the TME. Cell-cell interaction analysis and trajectory inference from the spatial transcriptomic data will reveal interactions between tumor and immune cells, potentially identifying key signaling pathways or cellular crosstalk that contributes to immune evasion or response to therapy. The results will be interpreted in the context of known mechanisms of immune regulation and tumor progression in the BM TME.   This spatial analysis will be crucial for understanding the complex interplay between tumor cells and the immune system, ultimately informing the development of novel immunotherapies targeting melanoma BM.   The task has no relations with other research aims according to the input data, as indicated by the empty relations array.
#### Aim 2: Preclinical screening of cytokines in orthotopic immunocompetent BM models
This research aim, "Preclinical screening of cytokines in orthotopic immunocompetent BM models," hypothesizes that specific cytokines can modulate the immune activity within the brain metastasis (BM) tumor microenvironment (TME) to enhance anti-tumor activity.   The primary goal is to identify and validate these cytokines for their potential to shift the BM TME from an immunologically "cold" state to a "hot" state, thereby improving the efficacy of immunotherapies. This will be achieved through a three-task approach. Task 2.1, "Screening of cytokines in BM TME," will utilize an in-house cytokine library to screen for candidates that influence immune activity in the BM TME. Task 2.2, "In-vitro validation of cytokines," will validate the identified cytokines using in-vitro models to confirm their impact on the BM TME.   This validation will focus on the modulatory effects on immune activity observed in Task 2.1.   Finally, Task 2.3, "In-vivo validation of cytokines," will employ single-cell analysis within in-vitro and in-vivo functional screening systems to assess the activity of myeloid, NK, and T cells for trans-acting MiTEs.   This in-vivo validation builds upon the in-vitro findings from Task 2.2.   The expected outcome is the identification of potent cytokines capable of reactivating the immune compartment within the BM TME, leading to the eradication of melanoma BMs.   These potent cytokines will subsequently be incorporated into antibody-cytokine fusion proteins (immunocytokines) targeting tumor antigens in Aim 3.   The relations between these research tasks are explicitly outlined in the research plan: Task 2.2 depends on the results of Task 2.1, and Task 2.3 utilizes the validated cytokines from Task 2.2.   This aim contributes to the broader project goal of developing AI-tailored immunocytokines to target melanoma brain metastases by providing a preclinical screening platform to identify promising cytokine candidates.
##### Research Tasks
##### Task 2.1: Screening of cytokines in BM TME
This research task, "Screening of cytokines in BM TME," aims to identify cytokines that modulate immune activity within the bone marrow tumor microenvironment (BM TME).   This will be achieved using our in-house cytokine library, a comprehensive collection of diverse cytokine proteins. The experimental design involves a systematic screening approach where each cytokine from the library will be individually assessed for its impact on immune cell activity within the BM TME.   The specific methodology will involve co-culturing primary immune cells isolated from the BM TME with individual cytokines from the library.   Immune cell activity will be measured by evaluating changes in cellular function, including but not limited to proliferation, cytotoxicity, cytokine secretion, and expression of activation markers.   These changes will be evaluated using established immunological assays such as flow cytometry, ELISA, and multiplex cytokine assays.   Data collected from these experiments will include cell counts, cell viability, cytokine levels, and the expression levels of surface and intracellular markers.   The analysis and interpretation framework will involve statistical comparisons of immune cell activity between cytokine-treated groups and untreated controls.   Statistical significance will be determined using appropriate statistical tests, such as t-tests or ANOVA, adjusted for multiple comparisons.   The results will be interpreted in the context of the known immunological functions of each cytokine and the broader literature on immune regulation within the tumor microenvironment.   This rigorous and comprehensive approach will enable identification of both immunostimulatory and immunosuppressive cytokines within the BM TME. The research task has no documented relations to other research aims.
##### Task 2.2: In-vitro validation of cytokines
Task 2.2, titled "In-vitro validation of cytokines," aims to validate the cytokines identified in Task 2.1 using in-vitro models. This task builds upon the cytokine candidates identified in Task 2.1, which focuses on designing fusion proteins and cleavage sites. The experimental design for Task 2.2 will involve creating cytokine-secreting clones of brain tropic melanoma cell lines through conditional (dox-induced) expression of a broad spectrum of promising BM immune re-activation candidate cytokines. These clones will be used to create tumor spheroids containing one or multiple cytokine cassettes and co-cultured with immune cells. By adding doxycycline, varied cytokine combinations will be tested. The in-vitro validation will confirm the modulatory effects of the identified cytokines on immune activity in the BM TME. Data collection will involve measuring the impact of cytokine combinations on immune cell activity within the co-culture system. Specific measurements will include assessing changes in immune cell activation, proliferation, and cytokine production in response to the introduced cytokines. The results analysis and interpretation framework will focus on comparing the effects of different cytokine combinations on immune cell activity. Statistical analysis will be performed to determine the significance of observed changes. The most effective cytokines and cytokine combinations identified in-vitro will be shortlisted for in-vivo testing in subsequent tasks, as described in Aim 2 and Task 2.5.   Based on the cytokine candidates identified in Task 2.1, Task 2.2 will perform in-vitro validation to confirm their modulatory effects on immune activity in the BM TME.
##### Task 2.3: In-vivo validation of cytokines
Task 2.3, "In-vivo validation of cytokines," aims to validate the cytokines selected in Task 2.2 using an in-vivo functional screening system focusing on myeloid, NK, and T cell activity for trans-acting MiTEs.   This task builds upon the in-vitro validation performed in the preceding Task 2.2, where potential cytokine candidates were identified.   The in-vivo validation in Task 2.3 will provide critical insights into the efficacy and functionality of these selected cytokines within a living organism, offering a more realistic representation of their therapeutic potential. Utilizing single-cell analysis, the task will investigate the impact of the selected cytokines on the activity of myeloid, NK, and T cells, immune cell populations known to play crucial roles in anti-tumor immunity.   This granular level of analysis will allow for the precise assessment of how each cytokine modulates the behavior of these immune cells within the complex tumor microenvironment. The in-vivo validation will likely involve introducing the selected cytokines into an appropriate animal model, possibly through direct injection or via engineered cells expressing the cytokines. Subsequently, the activity of myeloid, NK, and T cells will be monitored using single-cell technologies such as scRNA-seq, CyTOF, or other high-throughput methods. This data will be analyzed to determine the extent to which each cytokine influences immune cell activation, proliferation, differentiation, and cytotoxic function.   The analysis will also evaluate cytokine effects on the tumor microenvironment including immune cell infiltration, cytokine production and tumor cell response. The specific animal model and experimental conditions for this in-vivo validation will depend on the nature of the cytokines being tested and the target disease. Following the in-vitro validation in Task 2.2, Task 2.3 will conduct in-vivo validation of the selected cytokines using single-cell analysis on myeloid, NK, and T cell activity for trans-acting MiTEs. This subsequent validation step aims to confirm the efficacy and mechanism of action of the selected cytokines within a living organism. The relation between these tasks is sequential, with Task 2.3 directly building upon the findings of Task 2.2. The success of Task 2.3 is contingent on the identification of promising cytokine candidates in Task 2.2.
#### Aim 3: Design of tumor-targeting immunocytokines
This research aim, "Design of tumor-targeting immunocytokines," hypothesizes that potent cytokines identified in Aim 2, which reactivate the immune compartment and eradicate melanoma BMs, can be incorporated into antibody-cytokine fusion proteins (immunocytokines) targeting species-conserved tumor antigens highly expressed in melanoma BMs. The general goal is to break through the classical dichotomy of response/no-response in melanoma BM treatment and move towards cycles of model-driven optimization to pinpoint the most effective constructs for the IND stage. This aim will leverage advanced technologies and murine models of melanoma BMs.   The methodology employed across all research tasks involves protein design, production, and in vitro validation. Specifically, the research tasks include: 1) designing fusion proteins and cleavage sites for the top 3-5 mAb-cytokine combinations identified in Aim 2 using advanced protein design techniques, including selection of peptide linkers, blocking moieties, TAM-specific cleavage sites, and computational optimization of protein structure and stability; 2) producing the 3-5 mAb-cytokine fusion proteins through a contract research organization (CRO) focusing on stable molecules with high protein expression and minimal aggregation; and 3) validating the immunocytokines in vitro through SPR, ELISA, cell-based binding, reporter assays, and co-culture assays with huMDMs, NK or T cells, and tumor cells to assess their impact on myeloid and lymphoid cell activity and their efficacy in inducing cytotoxic activity. The expected results include the design, production, and in vitro validation of several immunocytokine candidates. These constructs will be further optimized based on the results of these assays, ultimately leading to the selection of the most promising candidates for subsequent preclinical and clinical development. There are no relations with other research aims, as specified in the provided research aim JSON data.
##### Research Tasks
##### Task 3.1: Design fusion proteins and cleavage site
This research task focuses on designing optimal fusion proteins and selecting appropriate cleavage sites for the top 3-5 monoclonal antibody (mAb)-cytokine combinations identified in Aim 2. The objective is to enhance the efficacy of these combinations in targeting and eradicating melanoma brain metastases (BMs). This task will involve advanced protein design techniques, including the selection of suitable peptide linkers, blocking moieties, and tumor-associated macrophage (TAM)-specific cleavage sites.   Computational methods will be employed to optimize the structure and stability of the fusion proteins.   The design process will leverage data from Aim 2, specifically the preclinical *in vitro* and *in vivo* screening results of various cytokine combinations, to select the most effective combinations for fusion protein development. The selection of peptide linkers will consider factors like flexibility, stability, and immunogenicity. Blocking moieties will be incorporated to prevent non-specific binding and off-target effects.   TAM-specific cleavage sites will be chosen to ensure that the cytokine payload is released specifically within the tumor microenvironment. Computational optimization will involve molecular dynamics simulations and structure-based design to predict and enhance the stability and binding affinity of the fusion proteins.   The resulting fusion protein designs will be evaluated in subsequent tasks for their ability to induce cytotoxic NK and T cell activity against melanoma BMs, contributing to turning the "cold" BM TME "hot." The relations array is empty, indicating no direct dependencies on other research aims within the project. However, this task relies on the outcomes of Aim 2, where promising cytokine candidates are screened using *in vitro* tumor spheroid co-cultures with immune cells and *in vivo* testing in intracardial melanoma BM models.
##### Task 3.2: Produce fusion proteins
This research task, numbered 3.2, focuses on the production of 3–5 monoclonal antibody (mAb)-cytokine fusion proteins. These fusion proteins are crucial for investigating the efficacy of targeted immunocytokine therapies against melanoma brain metastases. The production process will be outsourced to a contract research organization (CRO) with a proven track record in high-quality and timely protein synthesis. Selection criteria for the CRO will be based on prior experience to ensure reliable delivery. The production process will encompass several critical steps to ensure the generation of stable and functional fusion proteins.   A key aspect of the production process involves selecting stable molecules. This selection will focus on identifying fusion proteins that exhibit high protein expression levels and minimal aggregation. High expression levels are essential for obtaining sufficient quantities of the fusion proteins for subsequent *in vitro* and *in vivo* studies.   Minimizing aggregation is crucial for maintaining the structural integrity and biological activity of the fusion proteins, ensuring accurate and reliable experimental results. The fusion proteins produced in this task will be subsequently employed in preclinical testing to evaluate their efficacy in reactivating the immune compartment within the brain metastatic microenvironment. These *in vitro* and *in vivo* tests (described in other tasks) will assess the fusion protein's ability to modulate immune cell activity and eradicate melanoma brain metastases. Ultimately, this task's output will be essential for the design and development of tumor-targeting immunocytokines (Aim 3). The relations array for this task is empty.
##### Task 3.3: In-vitro validation of immunocytokines
This research task, "In-vitro validation of immunocytokines," aims to validate the efficacy of engineered immunocytokines in modulating immune cell activity and promoting anti-tumor responses.   The central hypothesis is that these immunocytokines, comprising monoclonal antibodies fused to cytokines, can enhance interactions between myeloid and lymphoid cells, leading to increased tumor cell killing.   The experimental methodology involves a series of in vitro assays. First, the binding affinity and specificity of the immunocytokines to their target antigens will be confirmed using Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR), Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay (ELISA), cell-based binding assays, and reporter assays. These assays will provide quantitative and qualitative data on the interaction between the immunocytokines and their targets, confirming the intended binding characteristics.   Next, the impact of the immunocytokines on the interplay between myeloid and lymphoid cell activity will be evaluated using in vitro co-culture assays. Human monocyte-derived macrophages (huMDMs) will be co-cultured with Natural Killer (NK) cells or T cells in the presence of the immunocytokines.   These assays will assess changes in cytokine production, cell activation markers, and cell proliferation, providing insights into the immunomodulatory effects of the immunocytokines on immune cell interactions. Finally, the ability of the fusion proteins to induce cytotoxic activity in NK and T cells against tumor cells will be determined. Co-cultures of huMDMs, NK cells, or T cells with tumor cells will be treated with various mAb-cytokine chimeras. Cytotoxicity assays will be performed to measure tumor cell death in response to the immunocytokines.   Data collected from these assays will be analyzed to determine the efficacy of the different mAb-cytokine combinations.   This will involve comparing the results of the treated groups with untreated controls and assessing the statistical significance of the observed differences.   The analysis will focus on quantifying binding affinities, changes in cytokine levels, expression of activation markers, and the extent of tumor cell killing.   Interpretation of the results will consider the relative potency of the different immunocytokine constructs in modulating immune cell activity and inducing anti-tumor responses.   The empty "relations" array in the provided JSON indicates that this task does not have direct dependencies or interactions with other research aims within the larger project.
